A Representative Statewide Study of New Jersey Adults

eagletonpoll.rutgers.edu • poll@eagleton.rutgers.edu • 848-932-8940

The Eagleton Center for Public Interest Polling (ECPIP), home of the Rutgers- Eagleton Poll, was established in 1971. Now celebrating its 50th anniversary and publication of over 200 public opinion polls on the state of New Jersey, ECPIP was the first university-based statewide public opinion polling operation in the United States.

Our mission is to provide scientifically sound, non-partisan information about public opinion. ECPIP conducts research for all levels of government and nonprofit organizations with a public interest mission, as well as college and university-based researchers and staff. ECPIP makes it a priority to design opportunities for undergraduate and graduate students to learn how to read, analyze, design, and administer polls. We pride ourselves on integrity, quality, and objectivity.
To read more about ECPIP and view all of our press releases and published research, please visit our website: eagletonpoll.rutgers.edu.

APPENDIX A: SURVEY INSTRUMENT 93
The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel

The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel is the largest probability-based opinion sharing platform in New Jersey. It is comprised of a representative group of panelists randomly selected throughout New Jersey who have agreed to share their opinions through surveys.

The panel was established in 2023 as a joint research venture between The Eagleton Center for Public Interest Polling at the Eagleton Institute of Politics at Rutgers-New Brunswick (ECPIP) and SSRS. Both organizations are members of the AAPOR (American Association for Public Opinion Research) Transparency Initiative and committed to full compliance of AAPOR standards and transparency.
The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel Methodology

The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel is a probability-based panel of New Jersey adults aged 18 or older. Members are recruited randomly based on statewide representative ABS (Address Based Sample) design. ABS sample is drawn from the Delivery Sequence File (DSF) maintained by the U.S. Postal Service. Population coverage of the DSF is in the 98%-99% range.

During the recruitment process, demographic information on panelists is collected. This data is stored securely and used to determine eligibility for specific studies (if needed). We also rely on these data to improve the survey experience for panelists by avoiding the need to re-ask some demographic questions with each survey.
How are Garden State Panel surveys conducted?

The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel is a multi-mode panel (web and phone). Most panelists take self-administered web surveys; however, the option to take surveys conducted by a live telephone interviewer is available to those who prefer to complete surveys by phone and/or who do not use the Internet. Panelists also have the option of taking surveys in their preferred language (English or Spanish). We translate and conduct surveys in Spanish as required by specific projects.

Samples are drawn among panel members to best meet the study criteria. Selected panelists are sent an email invitation to participate in the survey, including unique survey link. The link allows the respondent to complete the self-administered online survey. Our contact protocol also includes a text message survey reminder for panelists that have consented to receiving them. All telephone interviews are conducted live using a CATI (Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing) system. Interviewers ask to speak with the person at that number by name who is a member of the Garden State Panel.
SSRS web surveys comply with industry best practices. They are optimized for smartphone/mobile device administration and are adapted to modern operating systems and browsers.
How is the Garden State Panel an improvement over opt-in panels?

The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel is a fully probability-based panel because it recruits randomly selected panelists from a probability-based sample source that reflects the New Jersey adult population. Results obtained from this panel can statistically represent the target population with a known margin of error.

Unlike typical opt-in panels, no one can “volunteer” to be part of the Garden State Panel. They must be selected randomly and invited to participate. By employing rigorous sampling methods, we not only reduce the risk of selection bias but also minimize susceptibility to “bots” or fraudulent panelists. This additional layer of security enhances the reliability of our survey results, providing accurate insights into New Jersey’s diverse populations.
For more information, visit: https://eagletonpoll.rutgers.edu/gardenstatepanel/.
Methodology
Overview

This survey was conducted from December 29, 2025, to January 16, 2026, with a random sample of 2,089 New Jersey residents, aged 18 or older. Sample included a mixture of panelists derived from the Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel and the SSRS Opinion Panel, as well as a cell phone oversample from the L2 national voter registration database.

The survey was available in English on web and phone and in Spanish on web. English phone and Spanish web versions of the survey were only available to panelists in the Rutgers- Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel or the SSRS Opinion Panel. The Eagleton Center for Public Interest Polling (ECPIP) employed text-to-web recruitment methods in a supplemental oversample; this version of the survey was only available on the web in English. Ultimately, completes on this survey can be broken into three categories: calling with live interviewers in English (n=31), web-only English (n=2,025), and web-only Spanish (n=33). Distribution of mode in this sample is:
English Phone 1% English Web 97% Spanish Web 2%

The survey was fielded by SSRS through the Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel and SSRS Opinion Panel via web and phone. The Eagleton Center for Public Interest Polling (ECPIP) fielded supplemental oversampling via text-to-web recruitment, utilizing sample from L2, Inc. The questionnaire was developed by ECPIP in collaboration the Robert Wood Johnson Foundation (RWJF) and alignco. All data analyses were completed in house by ECPIP. This survey was paid for and sponsored by RWJF. ECPIP is based at the Eagleton Institute of Politics at Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey, a non-partisan academic center for the study of politics and the political process. For more information, please contact poll@eagleton.rutgers.edu.
Sample Design

About the Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel members were recruited randomly via an addressbased sample (ABS) survey of New Jersey residents aged 18+. ABS respondents are randomly sampled by Marketing Systems Group (MSG) through the U.S. Postal Service’s Computerized Delivery Sequence File (CDS), a regularly updated listing of all known addresses in the U.S. For the SSRS/Rutgers-Eagleton Garden State Panel, known business addresses are excluded from the sample frame. Respondents of the original survey were asked if they would like to be invited to further surveys, and those who indicated yes were added to the Garden State Panel.

The Rutgers-Eagleton/SSRS Garden State Panel is a multi-mode panel (web and phone). Most panelists take self-administered web surveys; however, the option to take surveys conducted by a live telephone interviewer is available to those who do not use the internet as well as those who use the internet but are reluctant to take surveys online.

About the SSRS Opinion Panel SSRS Opinion Panel members are recruited randomly based on nationally representative ABS design, including Hawaii and Alaska. ABS respondents are randomly sampled by Marketing Systems Group (MSG) through the U.S. Postal Service’s Computerized Delivery Sequence File (CDS), a regularly updated listing of all known addresses in the U.S. For the SSRS Opinion Panel, known business addresses are excluded from the sample frame. Additional panelists are recruited via random digit dial (RDD) telephone sample of cell phone numbers connected to a prepaid cell phone. This sample is selected by MSG from the cell phone RDD frame using a flag that identifies prepaid numbers. Prepaid cell numbers are associated with cell phones that are “pay as you go” and do not require a contract.

The SSRS Opinion Panel is a multi-mode panel (web and phone). Most panelists take selfadministered web surveys; however, the option to take surveys conducted by a live telephone interviewer is available to those who do not use the internet as well as those who use the internet but are reluctant to take surveys online.

About L2 L2, Inc. is a national voter data company that maintains a comprehensive database of registered voters across the United States. The database integrates individual-level information from state voter files with appended demographic, household, and vote history data.

Survey Sampling Sample drawn for this study from the SSRS/Rutgers-Eagleton Garden State or SSRS Opinion Panel Panelists included New Jersey residents ages 18 or older. Sample was drawn using a probability proportional to size (PPS) methodology to ensure adequate representation of each demographic group while minimizing the variability of the final weights. The sample was additionally stratified by preferred survey language, mode, and race to meet the sample size targets for each group.

The oversample was drawn in part from the L2 national voter database. The oversample frame was built from national voter files maintained by L2 and designed to allow for oversamples of Black, Hispanic, and Asian respondents. Respondents’ race and ethnicity were identified through L2’s modeled race and ethnicity variables, and an oversample of Black, Hispanic, and Asian respondents was created. The sample was drawn from records that have a landline or cell phone number appended.
Weighting

Data were weighted to represent the residential adult population of New Jersey. Weighting was accomplished by applying base weights, making an adjustment to account for overlapping sample frames, and calibrating sample demographic distributions to match target population benchmarks.

Base Weight The first step in the weighting is to apply a base weight that accounts for the recruitment sampling probabilities and respondent selection. Three samples were pulled for this study: a PPS sample from the SSRS Opinion Panel, a PPS sample from the Garden State Panel, and a client provided sample from L2.
Garden State Panel Base Weight The sample was selected based on a stratified probability-proportionate-to-size (PPS) sampling allocation where the measure-of-size (MOS) is computed as:
𝑁ℎ
𝑀𝑂𝑆ℎ = ∑ √𝐹𝑊ℎ𝑖
where 𝐹𝑊ℎ𝑖 is the final calibrated full-population weight based on the entire Garden State Panel for panelist i in stratum h.

Strata h was formed by dividing the full panel into equally sized strata based on 𝐹𝑊ℎ𝑖 and selecting replicates across strata such that the resulting samples were proportionate by strata based on the MOS.

The base weight accounts for the panelists’ probability of selection into the sample using the following formula: 𝐵𝑊 = 𝐹𝑊ℎ𝑖 × (𝑁ℎ⁄𝑛ℎ) where 𝐹𝑊ℎ𝑖 is the panelist weight described above, 𝑁ℎ is the size of stratum h and 𝑛ℎ is the number of panelists selected from stratum h. These weight-based strata were crossed with preferred survey language, mode, and race to meet the required sample size targets for each group.

SSRS Opinion Panel Base Weight The Panel-wide base weight adjusts for the SSRS Opinion Panel recruitment and retention process—specifically, differential probabilities of being selected for the recruitment sample, completing the registration survey, joining the Panel, and remaining on the Panel.

The design weight accounts for differential probabilities of selection for the recruitment sample. The design weight for the SSRS Opinion Panel was computed differently depending on whether the panelist was recruited from address-based sample (ABS), a prepaid cell sample, or the SSRS dual-frame RDD telephone Omnibus.
The design weight for ABS recruits corrects for the disproportionate ABS design by adjusting the distribution of sample across the ABS strata to match the distribution of the ABS frame across strata.

ABS recruits come from a variety of sample sources, some of which employ different stratification schemes. The design weight for ABS recruits is tailored to the stratification scheme used for the sample from which the panelist was recruited. Currently, ABS recruitment waves for the SSRS Opinion Panel are stratified on a combination of geographic region and modelbased indicators of the presence of key subpopulations.
The design weight for prepaid cell recruits accounts for any disproportionate sampling of prepaid cell phone numbers from the cell phone RDD frame.

The design weight for the telephone Omnibus recruits is their original base weight computed at the time of the original omnibus interview. This base weight accounts for selection probabilities associated with the overlapping dual-frame Omnibus sample design.1 This base weight is a function of the landline and cell frame sample sizes as well as each respondent’s telephone usage and number of adults in the household.
Two adjustments are applied to the recruitment design weight:

items.text: A nonresponse adjustment correcting for variability in the recruitment response rate.
An attrition adjustment correcting for variability in the rate at which originally recruited panelists are retained on the Panel.

body.text: Both steps use a weighting class adjustment in which adjustment cells are defined by a cross of the recruitment channel and geographic strata.
For ABS recruits, a household size adjustment is also applied to correct for the sampling of one adult within each sampled address.

To create the final Panel-wide base weight, the full Panel is raked to target parameters for the population of U.S. adults (ages 18+). Panel-wide raking parameters include gender, age, educational attainment, race/ethnicity, Census division, civic engagement, population density, Internet use frequency, voter registration status, party identification, religion, household size, and home tenure. This raking step uses panelist profile variables; missing data in these variables is filled in using hot decking prior to raking.
The study-level base weight adjusts for differential probabilities of selection from the SSRS Opinion Panel into the sample for this specific study. The study-level base weight is calculated as:

Study-level sampling strata were formed from quantiles of PABW, with higher-weight panelists being given a higher probability of selection. These weight-based strata were crossed with preferred survey language, mode, and race to meet the required sample size targets for each group.
L2 Sample The L2 sample for this study was assigned a base weight of one.

Composite Adjustment A composite adjustment was applied to combine the Garden State Panel, Opinion Panel, and the L2 samples. The composite adjustment brings the proportion of the targeted groups in the combined sample to equal the proportion in the Garden State Panel and Opinion Panel samples. The composite adjustment can be expressed as 𝜆𝑖|𝐺𝑆𝑃,𝑂𝑃,𝐿2 = 𝑃𝑖|𝐺𝑆𝑃∪𝑂𝑃⁄𝑃𝑖|𝐺𝑆𝑃∪𝑂𝑃∪𝐿2, where 𝑃𝑖|𝐺𝑆𝑃∪𝑂𝑃 is the proportion of Garden State Panel and Opinion Panel interviews in stratum 𝑖 and 𝑃𝑖|𝐺𝑆𝑃∪𝑂𝑃∪𝐿2 is the proportion of interviews from the combined Garden State Panel, Opinion Panel, and L2 oversample in stratum 𝑖.

Final Base Weight The final base weight is the product of the initial base weight and the composite adjustment, 𝜆. 𝑑0𝐹𝐼𝑁𝐴𝐿 = 𝐵𝑊 × 𝜆 The final base weight was standardized to sum up to the number of completed interviews.
Calibration With the base weight applied, the data were weighted to balance the demographic profile of the sample to target population parameters.

Data were calibrated by raking sample distributions to target population distributions using iterative proportional fitting. This procedure balances each calibration variable to target benchmarks individually and iteratively. The entire set of calibration variables is cycled through until the weights converge across all dimensions.

Data were weighted to distributions of sex by age, sex by education, age by education, detailed education, race/ethnicity, region, home tenure, voter registration, number of adults per household, civic engagement, internet use frequency and 2024 presidential recalled vote, Race by Sex, and Race by Age. Missing data in the raking variables were imputed using hot decking.
Hot deck imputation replaces the missing values of a respondent randomly with another similar respondent without missing data. Hot decking was done using an SPSS macro detailed in
‘Goodbye, Listwise Deletion: Presenting Hot Deck Imputation as an Easy and Effective Tool for
Handing Missing Data’ (Myers, 2011).

Weights were trimmed at the 2nd and 98th percentiles to prevent individual interviews from having too much influence on survey-derived estimates.

Effects of Sample Design on Statistical Inference Post-data collection statistical adjustments require analysis procedures that reflect departures from simple random sampling. We calculate the effects of these design features so that an appropriate adjustment can be incorporated into tests of statistical significance when using these data. The so-called "design effect" or deffrepresents the loss in statistical efficiency that results from a disproportionate sample design and systematic non-response. 5 The total sample design effect for this survey is 2.62.
SSRS calculates the composite design effect for a sample of size n, with each case having a weight, w, as:6

All surveys are subject to sampling error, which is the expected probable difference between interviewing everyone in a population versus a scientific sampling drawn from that population. Sampling error should be adjusted to recognize the effect of weighting the data to better match the population. In this survey, the simple sampling margin of error is +/- 2.1 percentage points. With a design effect7 of 2.62, the adjusted margin of error is +/- 3.5 percentage points. Thus, if

50% of New Jersey adult residents in this sample favor a particular position, we would be 95% sure that the true figure is between 46.5% and 53.5% (50 +/- 3.5) if all residents had been interviewed, rather than just a sample. Margins of error for subgroups will be larger.

Sampling error is only one possible source of error in a survey estimate. Sampling error does not consider other sources of variation inherent in public opinion studies, such as selection bias, non-response bias, question wording, context effects, or reporting accuracy, which may contribute additional error.

Reported Analysis Text in the following report only reports on demographic relationships and comparisons deemed statistically significant based on chi-square analysis. Frequencies are reported for all questions; demographic comparisons only appear if proven statistically significant at the 95% confidence interval (p<.05), taking into account margin of error. Demographic groups are only included in analysis if they have an “n” (sample size) of at least 100; “n” changes on a questionto-question basis due to display logic and respondents being given the option to refuse to answer any individual question.

Crosstab analyses are only included for select demographics, based on standard ECPIP demographics. For the purpose of this report, in-text analyses are reported where significant based on: Party affiliation, gender, race and ethnicity, age, income, region, and educational attainment.
Where applicable, frequency comparisons are made between this survey and the 2022 health inequities survey.

KEY TERMS, DEFINITIONS, AND GROUPINGS

children.text: Race and Ethnicity

For purposes of analysis, respondents’ race or ethnicity were categorized as either white, Black, Hispanic or Latino, Asian, or “other.” The “other” category includes those who identify as multiracial. For example, if a respondent indicates they are white and Black, they would be placed in the “other” category. Those identified as white, Black, or Asian also identify as non- Hispanic or Latino. Analysis in this report focuses on white, Black, Hispanic or Latino, and Asian respondents.
Region

ECPIP typically divides the state of New Jersey into five regions: Urban, suburban, exurban, Philadelphia area/South Jersey, and shore, based upon the state’s counties. Urban includes Essex and Hudson Counties. Suburban includes Bergen, Mercer, Middlesex, Passaic, and Union Counties. Exurban includes Hunterdon, Morris, Somerset, Sussex, and Warren Counties. South Jersey near Philadelphia includes Burlington, Camden, Cape May, Cumberland, Gloucester, and Salem Counties. And shore includes Atlantic, Monmouth, and Ocean Counties.
Parent Status

Respondents were asked whether they are or someone else is currently pregnant with their child or if they have a biological child. Those who indicated any of those experiences applied to them were included in a subgroup of parents and future parents that were asked about their experiences and/or plans during pregnancy and childbirth. Collectively, this group is simply referred to simply as “parents,” regardless of whether or not their child was already born.

body.text: KEY TAKEAWAYS
Discrimination and Everyday Interactions

items.text: Most New Jerseyans do not frequently feel personally discriminated against because of their race or ethnicity, but experiences diverge sharply by race. A plurality of Black residents say they "sometimes" feel discriminated against both by other individuals and groups and by the state's laws and institutions (46% in both cases), while a majority of white residents say they "never" feel this way (42% by individuals, 51% by institutions).

Nearly 3 in 10 residents (29%) believe Black people in New Jersey face "a great deal" or "a lot" of discrimination, but views vary dramatically. Black residents are more than twice as likely as white residents to hold this view (56% vs. 22%), and Democrats are far more likely than Republicans to agree (40% vs. 15%).

When it comes to improving racial equality in the United States, residents place the most responsibility on the news media: 81% say the media has at least "some responsibility," more than any other institution or group tested, followed by the federal government (76%), the New Jersey state government (75%), and local government (70%).

body.text: Personal Health and Healthcare Access

items.text: New Jerseyans are generally able to get healthcare when needed, though mental health care lags significantly behind other services. At least 80% were able to get care when needed for prescriptions (88%), medical issues (83%), and dental issues (80%), but only 64% say the same for mental or emotional health care. Access disparities exist by income and education.
Residents tend to view their own community as a healthier place to live than New Jersey as a whole: 36% call their community "very healthy," compared with 18% who say the same about the state.

A majority of residents, 71%, believe some New Jerseyans have less of an opportunity to lead a healthy life than others, while 25% believe all residents have an equal opportunity. Democrats, Black residents, and Hispanic or Latino residents are more likely to perceive unequal opportunity.

Residents place primary responsibility for ensuring people can lead healthy lives on individuals themselves (87% say individuals have at least "some" responsibility), more than on any level of government. This tension between individual and structural responsibility runs throughout the survey's findings.

More than a third of residents (36%) believe one's own race or ethnicity has a "major influence" on a person's ability to lead a healthy life, with Black (46%) and Hispanic or Latino (42%) residents substantially more likely to say so than white residents, among whom a plurality say "minor influence" (43%).

body.text: Healthcare Inequities

items.text: Two in 5 New Jersey residents believe individuals are treated inadequately or unfairly in healthcare settings "all" or "most" of the time based on their health insurance status (41%), while 1 in 5 feel the same when it comes to race or ethnicity (20%), gender identity (16%), or sexual orientation (12%).

A quarter of residents believe racial discrimination interferes with access to quality healthcare in New Jersey at least "a good amount," with Black residents substantially more likely to hold this view (45%) than white (22%), Hispanic or Latino (34%), or Asian (18%) residents.

body.text: Housing

items.text: Homeownership rates divide sharply along racial and income lines. 80% of white residents own a home, compared with 46% of Black residents and 46% of Hispanic or Latino residents. Feelings of neighborhood safety follow a similar pattern: 66% of white residents feel "very safe" in their neighborhood, compared with 40% of Black residents.

Housing barriers are widespread: one-third of New Jerseyans have faced barriers obtaining affordable housing, 3 in 10 have faced barriers obtaining desirable housing, and 16% have faced barriers obtaining safe housing. These disparities are pronounced across party affiliation, gender, race and ethnicity, age, and income.

Among those who have faced housing barriers, cost overwhelmingly dominates as the primary obstacle: 70% cite cost as the most significant barrier, while only 3% cite discrimination. This framing of housing as primarily a cost problem, rather than a discrimination problem, is notable given the racial disparities in homeownership documented in the same survey.

More than 8 in 10 residents believe housing costs in their community make it difficult for some residents to pay for other necessities, and 78% say some New Jerseyans have a harder time obtaining affordable housing than others.

Residents place primary responsibility for ensuring access to safe and affordable housing on state, local, and federal governments, and one-third say increasing homeownership opportunities for all should be the state's top housing priority.

body.text: Maternal and Child Health

• Most parents report having had regular access to medical care during pregnancy and rate the quality of that care as "excellent," though perceptions of care quality differ by gender, race and ethnicity, and income. White residents are more likely than Black (48%) and Hispanic or Latino (49%) residents to rate their prenatal care as "excellent" (68%).

items.text: Six in 10 residents, regardless of parental status, believe some pregnant people have better access to quality prenatal care than others in New Jersey. Democrats, women, and higher-income residents are more likely to perceive this inequity.

Three in 10 residents say race or ethnicity has a "major impact" on infant mortality, but views are sharply divided. Black residents are about twice as likely as residents of other races and ethnicities to say race or ethnicity has a major impact (56% vs. 27% of white residents). A plurality of Republicans say race has no impact (36%), while a majority of Democrats say it has a major impact (39%).

When asked about causes of the higher Black infant mortality rate in New Jersey, residents are far more likely to attribute it to individual factors than to systemic ones. 71% say poor health of the person carrying the child is a cause and 64% cite personal choices of parents or caregivers, while only 40% attribute it to bias of healthcare professionals or the healthcare system. Black residents are much more likely to identify systemic bias as a cause (69% vs. 32% of white residents).

body.text: Policy Proposals

items.text: Thirty-eight percent of New Jerseyans say New Jersey's Family Leave Insurance Program does not go far enough, while 40% say it does just the right amount and 12% say it goes too far. Women and Democrats are more likely to say the program falls short.

New Jerseyans broadly and strongly support policies aimed at improving maternal and infant health outcomes. Strong majorities "strongly support" increasing availability of contraception or birth control methods (65%), an annual state review of maternal and infant mortality causes and rates (60%), and expanding access to health coverage for those uninsured during and after pregnancy (57%), among other approaches tested.

Nearly half of residents say Medicaid should be expanded to cover more people (47%), a view held more strongly by Democrats, Black residents, Hispanic or Latino residents, residents under 50, and urban residents. By contrast, 18% say Medicaid should be cut.

Residents are more supportive of Medicaid work requirements than of SNAP work requirements. 54% support the recently passed federal Medicaid work requirement law, while 49% oppose the law's new SNAP work requirements. Adding information about how many people stand to lose benefits moves opinion only modestly.

body.text: DEI Rollbacks and Connection to Health Equity

items.text: Residents are nearly evenly divided on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) programs: 41% believe these programs help level the playing field for people who have been denied equal opportunities, while 39% say they create unfair discrimination, and 20% are unsure. Democrats, women, Black residents, and college-educated residents are more likely to take the supportive view.

Over half of residents express concern about the impact of DEI rollbacks on the healthcare system and health outcomes, with Democrats, women, Black residents, residents 65 and older, and urban residents most likely to be "very concerned."

body.text: Systemic Discrimination and Reparations

items.text: Three in 10 New Jerseyans believe the legacies of slavery and other past discrimination have no impact on the position of Black people in the state today (29%), a view that divides sharply along partisan lines. Half of Black residents say these legacies affect Black New Jerseyans' position "a great deal" today (51%), compared with 39% of white residents who say there is no impact.

A majority of residents oppose the state providing reparations to Black residents for the injustices of slavery and discriminatory policies (54% opposed, including 42% strongly opposed), while 33% are supportive. Black residents are the only racial or ethnic group where a majority strongly supports reparations (58%), compared with 5% of white residents who strongly support them.

body.text: REPORT
Everyday Interactions

A majority of New Jerseyans have some level of personal contact with people who are of a different race or ethnicity from themselves in their daily life; half say they have “a lot” of contact (50%), 31% say “some,” and 17% “only a little.” One percent say they do not have any daily contact at all with those who are of a different race or ethnicity.

Age Residents 65 and older were less likely than their younger counterparts to say they have “a lot” of contact with people who are of a different race or ethnicity from themselves in their daily life (31%).
Perceptions of Discrimination and Racism

Overall, most New Jersey residents do not often feel personally discriminated against because of their race or ethnicity by either other individuals and groups or New Jersey’s laws, systems, policies, and institutions. Thirty percent “never” feel discriminated against by other individuals and groups, 37% “rarely,” 26% “sometimes;” 4% feel discriminated against “most of the time” and 1% “all of the time.” When it comes to the state’s laws, systems, policies, and institutions, 38% say they “never” feel discriminated against because of their race or ethnicity, 29% “rarely,” and 24% “sometimes;” 3% feel discriminated against “most of the time” and 2% “all of the time.”
Gender Men are more likely than women to say they “never” feel discriminated against due to their race or ethnicity by New Jersey’s laws and systems (42% vs. 35%, respectively).

Race and Ethnicity Responses regarding feeling personally discriminated against vary by race and ethnicity. Most white residents “never” feel discriminated against due to their race by either other individuals and groups (42%) or the state’s laws and institutions (51%). In either case, a plurality of Black residents say they “sometimes” feel discriminated against due to their race (46% in both circumstances). Responses are less definitive among Hispanic or Latino residents; 37% say they
“rarely” feel discriminated against by other individuals and groups and 34% say they
“sometimes” feel discriminated against by New Jersey’s laws and systems. Half of Asian residents say they “rarely” feel discriminated against by other individuals and groups (50%).
Asian residents’ feelings on discrimination by the state’s laws are less definitive; 34% say they
“rarely” feel discriminated against due to their race or ethnicity in this instance.

body.alt: Figure 2: Racial Discrimination by Other Individuals and Groups by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 2: Racial Discrimination by Other Individuals and Groups by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Income Likelihood of saying one has “never” felt discriminated against due to their race or ethnicity by New Jersey’s laws and systems increases with income.

New Jerseyans’ general perceptions of racial or ethnic discrimination by individuals or groups in the state varies based on the group in question. Eleven percent feel Black people in New Jersey are discriminated against “a great deal,” 18% “a lot,” 27% “a moderate amount,” 24% “a little,” and 12% “not at all.” Seven percent think Hispanic or Latino people in New Jersey are discriminated against “a great deal,” 15% “a lot,” 29% “a moderate amount,” 27% “a little,” and 13% “not at all.” Perceptions of racial or ethnic discrimination are lower for Asian people and especially white people in the state. Three percent think Asian people in the state are discriminated against “a great deal,” 6% “a lot,” 27% “a moderate amount,” 33% “a little,” and 18% “not at all.” And three percent feel white people in New Jersey are discriminated against “a great deal,” 3% “a lot,” 12% “a moderate amount,” 30% “a little,” and 45% “not at all.”
Party Affiliation Democrats (40%) are more likely than independents (24%) or Republicans (15%) to believe Black people face “a great deal” of or “a lot” of discrimination in New Jersey.
Democrats (28%) and independents (22%) are more likely than Republicans (13%) to believe Hispanic or Latino people are discriminated against “a great deal” or “a lot” in New Jersey.
Gender Women are more likely than men to say Black (32% vs. 25%, respectively) and Hispanic or Latino people (26% vs. 19%) are discriminated against “a great deal” or “a lot” in New Jersey.

Race and Ethnicity Black residents are more likely to say Black people in the state are discriminated against “a great deal” or “a lot” (55%), as compared with white (21%), Hispanic or Latino (30%), and Asian (26%) residents.

body.alt: Figure 3: Discrimination Against Black People by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 3: Discrimination Against Black People by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Hispanic or Latino (38%) and Black (32%) residents are more likely than white (17%) and Asian (16%) residents to say Hispanic or Latino people face “a great deal” of or “a lot” of discrimination.

body.alt: Figure 4: Discrimination Against Hispanic or Latino People by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 4: Discrimination Against Hispanic or Latino People by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Age Residents 49 and younger are more likely than those 50 and older to believe Black people are discriminated against “a great deal” or “a lot” in New Jersey.

When it comes to improving racial equality in the United States, residents place most of the onus on the news media (81% say the media has at least “some responsibility” to improve relations), followed by the federal government (76%), New Jersey state government (73%), residents’ local government (70%), individual people of color (67%), white individuals (66%), businesses (66%), and nonprofits and other organizations (61%).

body.alt: Figure 5: Responsibility for Improving Racial Equality in the US
body.title: Figure 5: Responsibility for Improving Racial Equality in the US

body.text: Party Affiliation Democrats are more likely than other partisans to say news media (66%), the federal government (73%), the New Jersey state government (58%), local government (52%), white individuals (46%), businesses (43%), and nonprofits (33%) have “a lot” of responsibility when it comes to improving racial equality in the country.

Gender Women are more likely than men to say news media (63% vs. 54%, respectively), the federal government (58% vs. 50%), the state government (49% vs. 42%), local government (47% vs. 38%), white individuals (38% vs. 28%), and businesses (38% vs. 30%) have “a lot” of responsibility in improving racial equality in the U.S.

Race and Ethnicity Black residents are more likely to say white people have “a lot” of responsibility in improving racial equality in the U.S. (50%) as compared with white (30%), Hispanic or Latino (33%), and Asian (31%) residents.
Black (46%), Hispanic or Latino (38%), and Asian (38%) residents are more likely than white residents (28%) to say businesses have “a lot” of responsibility.
Personal Health

Residents mostly feel positive about both their physical and their mental and emotional health. Twenty-one percent rate their physical health as “excellent” and 59% as “good;” 15% say their physical health is “only fair” and 3% rate it as “poor.” Thirty-two percent rate their mental and emotional health as “excellent” and 49% as “good;” 15% say it is “only fair” and 4% rate it as “poor.”
Political Affiliation Republicans are more likely to rate their mental health as “excellent” (43%) compared with Democrats (29%) and independents (29%).

Income A plurality of all income brackets say their physical health is “good,” however, residents earning less than $50,000 are less likely to label it “good” (45%) than those earning more. This group is more likely than their more affluent counterparts to say their physical health is “just fair” (27%).

Education Residents with a four-year college degree or additional schooling are more likely to label their physical health “excellent” than those with less education. However, a majority across levels of educational attainment say their physical health is “good.”

Within the previous 12 months, 83% of New Jerseyans report going to a regular check-up with a medical doctor, 71% have gone for a dental visit, 63% have gone for an eye exam, 38% have had a telehealth appointment, and 22% have spoken with a mental health professional.

Political Affiliation Republicans (91%) are more likely than Democrats (84%) and independents (78%) to have seen a medical doctor within the last year, meanwhile Democrats (27%) are more likely than Republicans (17%) and independents (20%) to have spoken with a mental health professional in the past year.
Independents (58%) are less likely than Democrats (66%) and Republicans (68%) to have gone for an eye exam.

Gender Women were more likely than men to have gone to the dentist (75% vs. 68%), spoken with a mental health professional (26% vs. 18%), done a telehealth appointment (42% vs. 33%), and gone for an eye exam (67% vs. 59%) within the last year.
Age Residents 65 and older are more likely than younger residents to have gone to a check-up with a medical doctor (96%) and gone for an eye exam (82%).
Residents 49 and younger are more likely than those 50 and older to have spoken with a mental health professional within the past year.
Income Likelihood of having seen a dentist within the last year increases with income.
Residents earning less than $50,000 are less likely than more affluent residents to have gone for an eye exam (51%).

Education Residents who have done postgraduate work are more likely to have gone to a regular check-up with a medical doctor (90%) and seen a dentist (86%) than those with less education. Having gone to the doctor or dentist increases with education.

Residents are generally able to get healthcare when needed. At least 80% were able to get care when needed for prescription fulfillment (88%), medical issues (83%), and dental issues (80%). Sixty-four percent say they were able to get care for mental or emotional health issues when they needed it.

body.title: Figure 6: Ability to Get Healthcare

body.text: Party Affiliation Independents are less likely than Democrats to say they were able to get care for mental or emotional issues when needed (54% vs. 72%, respectively).

Income Residents earning $100,000 to less than $150,000 in annual household income were more likely than those earning either less or more to say they were able to get care for medical issues when needed (92%).
Residents earning $100,000 or more are more likely than those earning less to say they were able to get care for prescriptions, dental, and mental or emotional issues when needed.

Education Residents with a four-year college degree or additional schooling are more likely than those with less education to say they were able to get care for dental issues and mental or emotional issues when needed.
Health in New Jersey

Residents tend to believe the community where they live is an overall heathier place to live than New Jersey as a whole. Thirty-six percent say their community is a “very healthy” place to live and 47% say it is “somewhat healthy.” Meanwhile, 18% say New Jersey is a “very healthy” place to live and 58% say “somewhat healthy.” These numbers are similar to resident sentiments from 2022.

body.alt: Figure 7: Healthiness of Local Community and New Jersey
body.title: Figure 7: Healthiness of Local Community and New Jersey

body.text: Age
Residents 65 and older are more likely than their younger counterparts to call the community where they live “very healthy” (48%).
A majority of residents believe some New Jerseyans have less of an opportunity to lead a healthy life than others – 71% say some have a harder time, while 25% believe all New
Jerseyans have an equal opportunity to live a healthy life.

body.alt: Figure 8: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Opportunity to Lead a Healthy Life
body.title: Figure 8: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Opportunity to Lead a Healthy Life

body.text: Numbers are largely unchanged from 2022; at that time, 70% said some have less of an opportunity and 27% said all residents had an equal opportunity.
Party Affiliation
Democrats (78%) and independents (72%) are more likely than Republicans (52%) to believe some New Jerseyans have less of an opportunity to lead a healthy life than others.
Race and Ethnicity Black (80%) and Hispanic or Latino (78%) residents are more likely to say some New Jerseyans have less of an opportunity compared with white (68%) and Asian (62%) residents.

Eighty percent or more of residents say access to affordable healthcare (83%), having a steady, good-paying job (82%), and personal health practices (80%) all have a “major influence” on a person’s ability to lead a healthy life. Seventy percent or more also say safe, affordable, stable housing (78%); the physical environment, including clean air and water (77%); access to healthy foods (76%); one’s own level of income (71%); and access to quality childcare and education (71%) all have a “major influence” on the ability to lead a healthy life. And sixty percent or more say community safety (68%), where a person lives (67%), access to safe places and green spaces to play and be physically active (64%), access to convenient and reliable transportation options (61%), and the amount of social support one has from family and friends (61%) have a “major influence.” And 36% of residents believe one’s own race or ethnicity has a “major influence” on a person’s ability to lead a healthy life.

These numbers are overall very similar to resident opinions in 2022, though belief that one’s level of income (65% in 2022 vs. 71% in 2025) and where a person lives (61% in 2022 vs. 67% in 2025) has a “major influence” on their ability to lead a health life grows slightly from 2022 to 2025.

body.alt: Figure 9: Influences on a Person’s Ability to Lead a Healthy Life
body.title: Figure 9: Influences on a Person’s Ability to Lead a Healthy Life

body.text: Party Affiliation
Democrats are more likely than other partisans to say access to affordable healthcare (91%);

having a steady, good-paying job (90%); safe, affordable, stable housing (85%); the physical environment (84%); access to healthy foods (83%); one’s income level (81%); access to quality childcare and education (83%); and access to convenient and reliable transportation options
(70%) have a “major influence” on a person’s ability to lead a healthy life.
Gender
Women are more likely than men to say one’s race or ethnicity (40% vs. 30%, respectively) has a “major influence” on a person’s ability to lead a healthy life.

Race and Ethnicity A plurality of Black (46%) and Hispanic or Latino (42%) residents say race or ethnicity has a “major influence” on one’s ability to lead a healthy life, while a plurality of white residents say “minor influence” (43%). Asian residents are more split – 41% say “major influence” and 39% say “minor.”

body.alt: Figure 10: Race or Ethnicity as an Influence on a Person’s Ability to Lead a Healthy Life by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 10: Race or Ethnicity as an Influence on a Person’s Ability to Lead a Healthy Life by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Age Residents aged 50 to 64 are less likely than those either younger or older to believe one’s income level has a “major influence” on their ability to lead a healthy life (56%).

Income Residents earning less than $50,000 in annual household income are less likely than more affluent residents to say having a steady, good-paying job has a “major influence” on a person’s ability to lead a healthy life (67%).
Education Belief that safe, affordable, and stable housing as a “major influence” on one’s ability to lead a healthy life increases with education.

Residents with a high school education or less are less likely than those with more schooling to say access to quality childcare and education has a “major influence” on the ability to lead a healthy life (53%).

New Jerseyans place most of the responsibility on individuals themselves for ensuring they have the ability to lead a healthy life (87% say individuals have at least “some” responsibility), followed by the federal government (83%), the state government (81%), and one’s own local government (78%). About two-thirds say businesses and corporations (67%) and community organizations (67%) have “some” responsibility when it comes to ensuring people have the ability to lead a healthy life.

body.alt: Figure 11: Responsibility for Ensuring People Have the Ability to Lead a Healthy Life
body.title: Figure 11: Responsibility for Ensuring People Have the Ability to Lead a Healthy Life

body.text: Party Affiliation Democrats are more likely than other partisans to say the federal government (67%), the state government (60%), and local government (50%) all have “a lot” of responsibility when it comes to ensuring people have the ability to lead a healthy life.
Democrats (34%) and independents (27%) are more likely to say businesses and corporations have “a lot” of responsibility as compared with Republicans (18%).

Gender Women are more likely than men to say the state government (53% vs. 42%, respectively) and local government (46% vs. 36%) have “a lot” of responsibility in making sure people have the ability to lead healthy lives.

Race and Ethnicity Hispanic or Latino (53%), Black (50%), and Asian (45%) residents are more likely than white residents (33%) to say one’s local government has “a lot” of responsibility in ensuring people can lead healthy lives.
Healthcare Inequities

Two in five New Jersey residents believe individuals in New Jersey are treated inadequately or unfairly in healthcare settings “all” or “most” of the time based on health insurance (41%), while one in five feel the same when it comes to race or ethnicity (20%); 16% feel this way regarding one’s gender identity and 12% say so based on sexual orientation. A plurality say an individual may “sometimes” be treated inadequately or unfairly because of each of these factors.

body.alt: Figure 12: Perceptions of Frequency of Mistreatment in Healthcare Settings
body.title: Figure 12: Perceptions of Frequency of Mistreatment in Healthcare Settings

body.text: Gender

Women are more likely than men to believe individuals in New Jersey are treated inadequately or unfairly “all” or “most” of the time because of their race or ethnicity (23% vs. 16%, respectively) and gender identity (18% vs. 13%).
Race and Ethnicity

Black and Hispanic or Latino residents are more likely than white and Asian residents to say individuals in the state are treated inadequately or unfairly “all” or “most” of the time in healthcare settings because of their race or ethnicity (39% and 28% vs. 13% and 18%).

body.alt: Figure 13: Perceptions of Frequency of Mistreatment in Healthcare Settings Due to Race or Ethnicity by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 13: Perceptions of Frequency of Mistreatment in Healthcare Settings Due to Race or Ethnicity by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Age Residents 49 and younger are more likely than those 50 and older to say individuals in the state are treated inadequately or unfairly “all” or “most” of the time in healthcare settings because of their race or ethnicity.
Belief that individuals are treated unfairly or unfairly “all” or “most” of the time because of their gender identity decreases as age increases.

Income New Jerseyans earning less than $50,000 are more likely than those earning more to say individuals in the state are treated inadequately or unfairly “all” or “most” of the time in healthcare settings because of their race or ethnicity (30%) and gender identity (26%).

Half of residents believe racial discrimination interferes with the ability of New Jersey residents to get quality healthcare in the state at least “some” (9% say “a great deal,” 17% “a good amount,” and 25% “just some”). Just over a third say racial discrimination interferes “only a little” (15%) or “not at all” (20%).

The number of residents who say racial discrimination interferes at least “some” is lower in 2025 than it was in the 2022 survey; at that time, 61% said racial discrimination interfered at least “some.”

Party Affiliation Democrats (37%) are more likely than independents (23%) and Republicans (13%) to say racial discrimination interferes with the ability of residents to get quality healthcare in the state at least “a good amount.”
Gender Women are more likely than men to say racial discrimination interferes with residents getting quality healthcare at least “a good amount” (33% vs. 20%, respectively).

Race and Ethnicity Black residents (46%) are more likely than white (21%), Hispanic or Latino (34%), and Asian (18%) residents to say racial discrimination interferes with residents getting quality healthcare in New Jersey at least “a good amount.”

body.alt: Figure 14: Perceptions of How Much Racial Discrimination Interferes with Obtaining Quality Healthcare by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 14: Perceptions of How Much Racial Discrimination Interferes with Obtaining Quality Healthcare by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Income Belief that racial discrimination interferes with residents getting quality healthcare either a “great deal” or “good amount” increases as income increases.
Housing

Sixty-eight percent of residents own a house, 7% rent a house, 15% rent an apartment, 2% rent rooms in a house or apartment, and 7% live with a relative or friend rent free. One percent do something else for housing and 1% do not currently have a permanent place to live.
Party Affiliation Republicans are more likely than either Democrats or independents to say they own a house (77% vs. 66% and 65%, respectively).
Race and Ethnicity White residents (80%) are more likely than Black (46%), Hispanic or Latino (46%), and Asian (70%) residents to say they own a house.

body.alt: Figure 15: Current Housing Situation by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 15: Current Housing Situation by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Age
Residents 18 to 34 are less likely to own a house (34%) as compared with those 35 and older.
Homeownership increases with age.
Income
Residents earning $50,000 or less in annual household income are less likely than those earning more to say they own a house (38%). Homeownership increases with income.

body.alt: Figure 16: Current Housing Situation by Income
body.title: Figure 16: Current Housing Situation by Income

body.text: Region
Residents living in New Jersey’s urban areas are less likely to say they own a house (38%)
compared with those living elsewhere in the state.
Education
Residents who have done some level of postgraduate work are more likely than those with less schooling to say they own a house (79%). Homeownership increases with education.
One-third of New Jerseyans have faced barriers obtaining affordable housing (33%), 3 in 10 have faced barriers obtaining desirable housing (29%), and 16% have faced barriers obtaining safe housing.

body.alt: Figure 17: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Housing
body.title: Figure 17: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Housing

body.text: Party Affiliation Independents and Democrats are more likely than Republicans to say they have faced barriers in obtaining both affordable (40% and 33% vs. 19%, respectively) and desirable (34% and 28% vs. 16%) housing.
Independents are more likely to say they have faced barriers in obtaining safe housing (21%) compared with Democrats (13%) and Republicans (12%).
Gender Women are more like to say they have faced barriers in obtaining affordable housing (37%) than men (29%).

Race and Ethnicity Black and Hispanic or Latino residents are more likely than white and Asian residents to say they have faced barriers obtaining affordable (44% and 47% vs. 28% and 27%, respectively) and desirable (38% and 40% vs. 23% and 26%) housing.

body.alt: Figure 18: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 18: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Age

A generational divide shows up in asking about barriers to housing. Residents 49 and under are more likely to say they have faced barriers obtaining affordable, desirable, and safe housing than those 50 and older. In the cases of affordable and safe housing, likelihood of having faced barriers decreases as age increases.

body.alt: Figure 19: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Age
body.title: Figure 19: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Age

body.text: Income

Likelihood of facing barriers obtaining affordable housing decreases about 10% among each income bracket analyzed for the purposes of this report, with half of those earning $50,000 saying they have faced such barriers (49%) compared with 20% of those earning $150,000 or more. Likelihood of facing barriers obtaining desirable housing also decrease as income increases; 40% of those earning $50,000 say they have faced such barriers compared with 18%
of those earning $150,000 or more.
Residents earning less than $50,000 are more likely than their more affluent counterparts to say they have faced barriers obtaining safe housing (32%).

body.alt: Figure 20: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Income
body.title: Figure 20: Experiences with Barriers to Obtaining Affordable Housing by Income

body.text: Among those who have faced some sort of issue in obtaining housing, the overwhelming majority (70%) say cost was or is the most significant barrier they have faced. Five percent point to availability, 5% say cost associated with “safer” areas, 3% say discrimination, 2% credit issues, and 1% income requirements. Twelve percent say something else.

A quarter of residents say they spend less than 20% of their total household monthly income on rent or mortgage payments (24%), 26% of residents say they spend 21-30%, 17% of residents spend 31-40%, 14% spend 41-50%, and 12% spend more than 50%.

body.alt: Figure 21: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage
body.title: Figure 21: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage

body.text: Numbers are fairly similar to 2022, though the 2025 survey sees a decrease in the number of residents who are spending less than 20% of their income on housing (30% in 2022 vs. 24% in 2025).
Gender Women (47%) are more likely than men (38%) to say they spend more than 30% of their household monthly income on rent or mortgage payments.

Age Residents 64 and younger are more likely than those 65 and older to say they spend at least 31% of their income on their rent or mortgage payments. Likelihood of spending 31% of income or more on rent or mortgage decreases with age. Residents 65 and older are the only age group where a plurality spend less than 20% of their income on rent or mortgage.

body.alt: Figure 22: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage by Age
body.title: Figure 22: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage by Age

body.text: Income

Residents who earn less than $150,000 in annual household income are more likely than those earning $150,000 or more to say they spend at least 31% of their income on their rent or mortgage payments. Likelihood of spending 31% of income or more on rent or mortgage decreases with income. Residents earning $150,000 or more are the only income bracket where a plurality spend less than 20% of their income on rent or mortgage.

body.alt: Figure 23: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage by Income
body.title: Figure 23: Percentage of Monthly Income Spent on Rent or Mortgage by Income

body.text: Residents generally feel safe in their neighborhoods – 57% feel “very safe” and 38% “somewhat safe,” while 4% say they feel “not very safe” and 1% do not feel safe at all. Numbers are largely unchanged from 2022.

body.alt: Figure 24: Feelings of Safety in Neighborhood
body.title: Figure 24: Feelings of Safety in Neighborhood

body.text: Overall feelings of safety were about the same in 2022, though higher on “very safe” at the time (64%) and lower on “somewhat safe” (31%).
Race and Ethnicity White residents are more likely to say they feel “very safe” in their neighborhood (66%) than Black (40%), Hispanic or Latino (47%), and Asian (49%) residents.
Age Feeling “very safe” in one’s neighborhood increase with age. New Jerseyans 50 and older are more likely than those 49 and younger to say they feel “very safe” in their neighborhood.

Income Feeling “very safe” also increases with income. Residents earning $100,000 or more in annual household income are more likely to say they feel “very safe” in their neighborhood compared with those earning less.
Region New Jerseyans living in the state’s urban regions are less likely than those living elsewhere to feel “very safe” in their neighborhood (40%).

body.alt: Figure 25: Feelings of Safety in Neighborhood by Region
body.title: Figure 25: Feelings of Safety in Neighborhood by Region

body.text: Just over 8 in 10 New Jerseyans believe housing costs in their local community make it difficult for some residents to pay for other necessities, like groceries or medical care (41% say “very difficult,” 41% “somewhat difficult”).

body.alt: Figure 26: Perceptions of Housing Costs Making It Difficult for Residents to Afford Other Necessities
body.title: Figure 26: Perceptions of Housing Costs Making It Difficult for Residents to Afford Other Necessities

body.text: Seventy-eight percent say some New Jerseyans have a harder time obtaining affordable housing than others.

body.alt: Figure 27: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Opportunity to Obtain Affordable Housing
body.title: Figure 27: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Opportunity to Obtain Affordable Housing

body.text: Party Affiliation Democrats (83%) and independents (79%) are more likely to say some New Jerseyans have a harder time obtaining affordable housing compared with Republicans (64%).
Gender Women (82%) are more likely than men (73%) to say some New Jerseyans have a harder time obtaining affordable housing than others.
Race and Ethnicity Black (86%) and Hispanic or Latino (85%) residents are more likely to say some have a harder time than others compared with white (76%) and especially Asian (65%) residents.

When it comes to ensuring access to safe and affordable housing in New Jersey, residents place most of the responsibility on the state government (84% say the New Jersey state government has at least “some responsibility”), residents’ local government (81%), and the federal government (80%). Seventy-two percent say private developers have at least “some responsibility.” Fewer residents place this level of responsibility on community members (50%) and nonprofit organizations (41%).

body.alt: Figure 28: Responsibility for Ensuring Safe and Affordable Housing
body.title: Figure 28: Responsibility for Ensuring Safe and Affordable Housing

body.text: Party Affiliation Democrats are more likely than other partisans to say the New Jersey state government (73%), local government (62%), the federal government (65%), and private developers (48%) have “a lot” of responsibility for ensuring access to safe and affordable housing in New Jersey.

Gender Women are more likely than men to say the state government (63% vs. 54%, respectively), local government (56% vs. 45%), the federal government (58% vs. 48%), and private developers (43% vs. 32%) have “a lot” of responsibility in ensuring safe and affordable housing access.

Age Residents 49 and younger are more likely than those 50 and older to say the New Jersey state government and local governments have “a lot” of responsibility in ensuring safe and affordable housing access.
Those 65 and older are less likely than younger residents to say private developers have “a lot” of responsibility (29%).
Education Residents with a high school diploma or less schooling are less likely to say the state government has “a lot” of responsibility (47%) than those with additional education.

One-third of New Jerseyans say the top housing priority for the state should be increasing home ownership opportunities for all (34%), followed by building more affordable housing (26%), and preventing homelessness (19%). Five percent say reforming zoning laws should be the top priority, 4% say addressing racial disparities in housing access, and 3% say preventing evictions. Nine percent are unsure.

body.alt: Figure 29: Top Housing Priority for New Jersey
body.title: Figure 29: Top Housing Priority for New Jersey

body.text: Race and Ethnicity Black residents put building more affordable housing as their top housing priority for the state (31%), while residents of other races and ethnicities coalesce around increasing homeownership opportunities.

Income Residents earning at least $100,000 in household income put increasing homeownership opportunities as their clear top priority, whereas those earning less than $50,000 say building more affordable housing. Those earning between $50,000 and less than $100,000 are more split between these two concepts.

Education Residents with some college education or additional schooling are more likely than those with a high school education or less (25%) to say increasing homeownership should be the state’s top priority.
Maternal/Child Health

Residents were asked whether they are or someone else is currently pregnant with their child or if they have a biological child. In total, 43% of residents were included in a subgroup of parents and future parents that were asked about their experiences and/or plans during pregnancy and childbirth. Hereafter, this group will be referred to simply as “parents,”
regardless of whether or not their child was already born.
Ninety-two percent of parents had or have regular access to medical care during pregnancy; 5%
did/do not have access to regular care, and 3% were unsure how to answer.

body.alt: Figure 30: Access to Regular Medical Care During Pregnancy
body.title: Figure 30: Access to Regular Medical Care During Pregnancy

body.text: This number is slightly lower than the 96% who said they had access to regular medical care during pregnancy in 2022.

Regarding the quality of medical care they received during pregnancy, 60% of parents say the care was/is “excellent,” 29% “good,” 7% “only fair,” 2% “poor,” and 2% are unsure of how to rate the care they received/receive. These ratings are similar to 2022.
Gender
Men are more likely than women to rate the quality of care as “excellent” (77% vs. 55%, respectively).

body.alt: Figure 31: Quality of Care During Pregnancy
body.title: Figure 31: Quality of Care During Pregnancy

body.text: Race and Ethnicity
White residents are more likely to rate the medical care they received during pregnancy as
“excellent” (68%) as compared with Black (48%) and Hispanic or Latino (49%) residents.

body.alt: Figure 32: Quality of Care During Pregnancy by Race and Ethnicity
body.title: Figure 32: Quality of Care During Pregnancy by Race and Ethnicity

body.text: Note: The n size for Asian respondents on this question was 96. Because n size was under 100, this group was not included in analysis.
Income
A clear majority of residents earning $50,000 or more in household income rate their care as
“excellent,” though those earning less are split – 41% of those earning less than $50,000 say the care was/is “excellent,” while 40% say “good.”

body.alt: Figure 33: Quality of Care During Pregnancy by Income
body.title: Figure 33: Quality of Care During Pregnancy by Income

body.text: When it comes to specific aspects of prenatal care, a majority of parents felt, or currently feel, like their concerns were/are listened to, they were/are treated with dignity and respect, and their provider responded/responds to health concerns they may have. Eighty-eight percent of parents say their healthcare provider “always” or “often” treated/treats with dignity and respect. Eighty-six percent say their healthcare provider “always” or “often” responded/responds to health concerns. And 83% say their healthcare provider “always” or “often” listened/listens carefully to their wishes and concerns.

Resident experiences with prenatal care differed from 2022 to 2025. Where in 2022 64% said their healthcare provider “always” listened to their wishes and concerns, this number dipped eight points to 56% in 2025. Similarly, 77% in 2022 said their healthcare provider treated them with dignity and respect, while in 2025, this number dropped by nine points to 68%. “Always” having a healthcare provider respond to health concerns also dropped nine points from 2022 (71%) to 2025 (62%).

All residents regardless of parental status were asked about beliefs on prenatal care and health equity in New Jersey. Six in 10 residents say some pregnant people have better access to quality prenatal care than others in New Jersey (61%).

body.alt: Figure 34: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Access to Quality Prenatal Care
body.title: Figure 34: Equality of New Jerseyans’ Access to Quality Prenatal Care

body.text: Party Affiliation

body.text: When it comes to causes behind the higher death rate of Black babies born in New Jersey, 70% of residents believe poor health of the person carrying the child is a cause, 64% believe personal choices of the parents or caregivers is a cause, 63% say lack of access to necessary resources, 60% say lack of access to prenatal care, and 40% say bias of healthcare professionals or the healthcare system.

Uncertainty of all of these factors being a cause behind the higher death rate of Black babies born in New Jersey grows from 2022 to 2025: The share who say “don’t know” for poor health of the person carrying the child grows by 10 points (12% in 2022 vs. 22% in 2025), personal choices of the parents or caregivers by 10 points (14% in 2022 vs. 24% in 2025), lack of access to necessary resources by 10 points (10% in 2022 vs. 20% in 2025), lack of access to prenatal care by nine points (12% in 2022 vs. 21% in 2025), and bias of healthcare professionals or system by 13 points (14% in 2022 vs. 27% in 2025).

Policy Proposals and Solutions

Regarding New Jersey’s Family Leave Insurance Program (NJFLIP), 38% of residents say the program does not go far enough, 12% say it goes too far, and 40% say it does just the right amount. These numbers are fairly similar to 2022, though uncertainty grows slightly in 2025 (5% said “don’t know” in 2022 vs. 10% in 2025).

Party Affiliation Democrats (45%) and independents (38%) are more likely than Republicans to say the Family Leave Insurance Program does not go far enough. Nearly half of Republicans say the program does just the right amount (48%).

body.title: Figure 42: Opinions on NJFLIP by Party Affiliation

New Jerseyans overall “strongly support” a number of approaches that could have an impact on maternal and infant health outcomes. Nearly two-thirds “strongly support” increasing availability and use of contraception or birth control methods (65%). Six in 10 “strongly support” an annual review of causes and rates of maternal and infant mortality in the state (60%). Fifty-seven percent are strongly supportive of expanding access to health coverage for those who are uninsured during pregnancy and after pregnancy for postpartum services and another 57% “strongly support” making leave policies mandatory for all workplaces. Half “strongly support” mandating that leave is equivalent to full pay (50%) and just under half “strongly support” increasing the capacity and quality of home-visiting programs for new parents and their infants (47%). Forty-two percent “strongly support” providing a longer period off from work and 35% “strongly support” expanding access to doulas.

This level of support is largely similar to support in 2022, though strong support decreases for expanding access to health coverage during and after pregnancy (65% in 2022 vs. 57% in 2025), increasing the capacity and quality of home-visit programs (59% in 2022 vs. 47% in 2025), and expanding access to doulas (42% in 2022 vs. 35% in 2025). Uncertainty on many of these measures, plus making leave policies mandatory for all workplaces and mandating leave pay is equivalent to full pay increases from 2022 to 2025.

Historical Discrimination and Reparations

Three in 10 New Jerseyans think the legacies of slavery and other past discrimination have no impact on the position of Black people in New Jersey today (29%). Thirteen percent believe there is little impact on Black people in the state today, 15% say “just some,” 18% say “a good amount,” and 16% say “a great deal.”

body.text: Conclusion

The findings of the 2025 RWJF Health Inequities Survey reveal that a majority of New Jersey residents recognize health outcomes are not equally distributed across the state's population, yet views on the causes of those disparities and the appropriate policy responses to them vary considerably by race and ethnicity, party affiliation, gender, income, and education.

On the question of what shapes a person's ability to lead a healthy life, large majorities point to access to affordable healthcare, having a steady, good-paying job, and personal health practices as having a "major influence." Fewer residents say one's own race or ethnicity has a "major influence," though this view is held more commonly by Black and Hispanic or Latino residents than by white residents. Similarly, while a majority of residents say some New Jerseyans have a harder time leading a healthy life than others, residents place the greatest share of responsibility for ensuring healthy lives on individuals themselves, more than on any level of government.

These patterns carry through to more specific findings. When asked about the causes of the higher death rate of Black babies born in New Jersey, residents are more likely to point to individual-level factors, such as poor health of the person carrying the child and personal choices of parents or caregivers, than to systemic ones, such as bias of healthcare professionals or the healthcare system. Black residents are substantially more likely than residents of other races and ethnicities to identify systemic bias as a cause, a gap that reflects a broader pattern throughout the survey in which Black residents are more likely than others to perceive structural drivers of health disparities.

Partisanship is a consistent dividing line across the survey's topics. Democrats are more likely than Republicans to say various levels of government bear significant responsibility for health and housing outcomes, to support Medicaid expansion, to say DEI programs help level the playing field, and to believe the legacies of slavery continue to affect the position of Black New Jerseyans today. On several questions, partisan differences exceed 30 to 40 percentage points. At the same time, broad bipartisan majorities support a number of specific policy interventions, including expanding access to health coverage during and after pregnancy, mandating workplace leave policies, and conducting annual reviews of maternal and infant mortality rates in the state, suggesting areas of potential common ground even amid wider disagreement on structural questions.

Racial and ethnic differences in perceptions are similarly pronounced. Black residents report higher rates of personal discrimination, greater belief in the structural causes of health disparities, deeper concern about the impact of DEI rollbacks on health outcomes, and stronger support for reparations than residents of other races and ethnicities. On the question of reparations specifically, Black residents are the only group where a majority strongly supports the policy (58%), while 60% of white residents are strongly opposed. These differences in perception and experience are themselves a substantive finding, as they speak to the degree to which the state's residents hold divergent understandings of how race shapes health, housing, and opportunity in New Jersey.
Taken together, these findings offer a detailed accounting of public opinion on health equity in

New Jersey at a moment when many of the policies and programs most relevant to that goal are under active national debate. The results document where residents agree, where they diverge, and how demographic characteristics shape those views, providing a foundation for researchers, advocates, policymakers, and communicators working to understand and address health inequities across the state.
